behaviors.description: Generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing generic response
behaviors.justification: This behavior tests whether the LLM actually understands email context and generates contextually appropriate responses. A mock LLM can be configured to always return context-aware text, passing the test while hiding real failures where the prompt engineering is poor or the LLM doesn't properly parse the email thread. The failure mode—'the LLM generates generic responses despite being given the email context'—cannot be caught by mocking. Real LLM inference is required to verify the model comprehends the specific details in the email and incorporates them into the reply.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a real email thread with specific details (sender name, project reference, prior decision mentioned, etc.). Call the draft generation with the real LLM. Assert that the output mentions these specific points by name or clear reference, not generic placeholders. Run multiple examples to account for LLM variance.

behaviors.description: Factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on the fact-checker's ability to semantically understand claims in the draft and compare them to KB entries. If the system uses an LLM for claim extraction and semantic matching, a mock LLM or deterministic comparison would hide the real failure: 'the LLM extracts the wrong claims or misses semantic contradictions.' The integration of fact-checking logic with a real KB can be tested at Integration level if the fact-extraction is rule-based, but semantic contradiction detection typically requires LLM-powered natural language understanding. A system running against a real KB still needs LLM calls to catch comprehension failures.
behaviors.test_description: Create a draft with a specific factual claim (e.g., 'We decided to switch to vendor X in Q3'). Create a KB entry that contradicts it (e.g., 'Vendor X was rejected due to cost'). Call the fact-checker with real LLM inference. Assert that the contradiction is flagged with justification. Test edge cases: synonymous claims (same meaning, different words), partial contradictions, and claims absent from KB.

behaviors.description: When knowledge base has no relevant entries, draft includes disclaimer rather than fabricating facts
behaviors.justification: This directly tests LLM hallucination behavior. The failure mode is 'the LLM generates confident but false information despite the prompt instructing it not to, and despite the KB being empty.' A mock LLM can always be configured to include the disclaimer, but a real LLM might still fabricate facts if the prompt isn't sufficiently strong or if temperature/sampling is misconfigured. This is a prompt engineering and model behavior problem that cannot be caught without running the real LLM against real or realistic adversarial scenarios.
behaviors.test_description: Create an email thread asking about a topic not covered in the KB (e.g., 'Should we use technology X?' when the KB has no entries mentioning technology X). Generate a draft with an empty or irrelevant KB. Assert that the output contains a disclaimer (e.g., 'I don't have information about this topic in our knowledge base') rather than fabricated reasoning. Run multiple queries to account for hallucination variance.

behaviors.description: Approval workflow blocks sending until user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically
behaviors.justification: This is a state machine / business logic test with no LLM dependency. The failure modes—'send happens without approval,' 'approval state is lost,' 'wrong draft is sent'—are deterministic bugs in control flow that can be caught by unit tests. We mock the user approval input and the email sending service, then verify that send is only called when approval state is true. A unit test with good mocks of approval state and email service is sufficient to catch real bugs like unprotected code paths, race conditions at a local level, or missing approval checks.
behaviors.test_description: Create a draft in memory. Unit test: mock approval state as false, call send(), assert email service is not invoked. Mock approval state as true, call send(), assert email service is invoked exactly once with the correct draft. Test state transitions: verify approval can only be set once, cannot be revoked after sending, and that concurrent send attempts are serialized or rejected.

behaviors.description: Full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread
behaviors.justification: This test orchestrates multiple agentic steps in sequence: (1) LLM generates draft from email, (2) LLM performs fact-checking against KB, (3) workflow routes the result to approval. Each step may produce non-deterministic LLM outputs that affect downstream steps. A lower level test would mock one or both LLM calls, hiding integration failures between steps or drift in LLM outputs across the pipeline. Real production failures include 'draft generation hallucinates facts that fact-check then fails to catch,' or 'edge case in email parsing causes fact-check to receive malformed context.' The minimum infrastructure is a full pipeline test with real LLM calls.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a realistic multi-turn email thread with context, prior decisions, and queries. Execute the full pipeline: (1) parse email, (2) generate draft with real LLM, (3) extract draft text and run fact-check with real LLM against KB, (4) verify fact-check flags any issues or clears the draft, (5) assert draft is held for approval (not auto-sent). Verify the approval state carries the correct draft and sending happens after approval. Test realistic failure cases: email with conflicting information, email asking about a sensitive topic, email querying the KB about something not present.
